title: Food preservation to reduce waste
subtitle: Practical methods for extending the life of surplus seasonal produce
excerpt: UK households waste approximately 6.4 million tonnes of food per year, most of it avoidable. Preservation methods (freezing, lacto-fermentation, dehydrating, pickling) extend the useful life of seasonal gluts and reduce the waste fraction of the weekly shop.
sourceNotes: WRAP Household Food Waste report (2023). Food Standards Agency guidance on home preservation safety. National Centre for Home Food Preservation guidelines on lacto-fermentation and water-bath canning.

glossaryTerms.definition: Keeping materials and products in use. Food waste is one of the highest-carbon waste streams: approximately 8-10% of global greenhouse gas emissions are linked to food that is produced but not eaten.
The carbon embedded in the production of food includes land use, transport, processing, and refrigeration. When food is wasted, that embodied carbon is released without any nutritional benefit.

content.text: Food waste in UK households generates approximately 25 million tonnes of CO2 equivalent per year. The

of uneaten food (the energy, water, land, and transport invested in its production) is lost entirely when it goes to the bin. Preservation does not recover that carbon, but it does prevent the additional cycle of production required to replace what was wasted.

Freezing is the most versatile preservation method. Most vegetables benefit from blanching (2-3 minutes in boiling water, then immediate transfer to iced water) before freezing, which deactivates enzymes that cause texture and colour deterioration. Exceptions: onions, peppers, and herbs freeze raw successfully. Package in portions that match likely use. Label with content and date: frozen food has best quality within 3-12 months depending on the item, though remains safe indefinitely if kept frozen.

Lacto-fermentation uses salt to create an anaerobic brine in which lactobacillus bacteria multiply and acidify the vegetables to a safe, shelf-stable pH. The method requires no heat and no specialist equipment. Basic method: slice vegetables, salt at 2-3% by weight (20-30 g per kg), pack tightly into a jar, push down until brine covers the vegetable surface, and weight to keep vegetables submerged. Leave at room temperature for 3-10 days (taste daily). Move to the fridge when acidity and texture are to your liking. Sauerkraut, kimchi, pickled cucumbers, and fermented carrots all use this method.

Hot vinegar pickling (acidified water bath) produces pickles with a longer pantry shelf life than lacto-fermentation (12 months+ if sealed and heat-processed) but the vinegar flavour is more pronounced. Requires sterilised jars and a vinegar concentration of at least 5% acidity. Suitable for most hard vegetables: onions, beetroot, green beans, gherkins, and mixed vegetables.

Removing moisture to below 10% prevents microbial growth. A domestic food dehydrator operates at 40 to 70°C over 4 to 16 hours. The oven at its lowest setting (50-60°C) with the door slightly ajar works as a substitute. Best applications: tomatoes, mushrooms, herbs, apple rings, and chilli peppers. Dehydrated produce reduces to approximately 10% of its original weight and volume, making storage easy.

The highest-impact items to preserve are those with the highest production carbon and water footprint, those bought in surplus, and those most commonly wasted. Bread (freeze in slices), cooked meals (freeze in portions), seasonal vegetables in glut, and surplus fruit are consistently the largest household food waste categories in the UK. A simple habit of freezing half a loaf of bread rather than letting it go stale saves more over a year than any specialist preservation project.

attrs.title: Circular economy position of food preservation
attrs.body: In the waste hierarchy for food, preservation sits between prevention and redistribution. It cannot prevent the production carbon already spent, but it maximises the use of that production. The higher-order goal is to buy and grow only what will be eaten. Preservation handles the inevitable surplus, particularly from seasonal garden produce where volumes cannot be controlled precisely.

content.text: value of food preservation is in keeping the nutritional value of food in use: the energy and resources invested in growing it are not wasted, and no additional resources are needed to replace what would have been thrown away.
